Good design isn't about making things look beautiful — it's about making them make sense.

## The wrong question

When most people evaluate a design, they ask: *does it look good?* That's a reasonable start, but it's the wrong primary question. The right question is: *does it make sense?*

Beauty in design is a byproduct of clarity, not the goal. When every element is exactly where it should be, doing exactly what it should do, the result tends to look good almost automatically. When you chase aesthetics for their own sake, you often get something that looks impressive in a screenshot and frustrates people in actual use.

## What clarity means in practice

Clarity is about reducing the cognitive load of understanding. It shows up in small decisions:

- Labels that say what they mean, not what sounds clever
- Buttons that are obviously clickable
- Errors that explain what went wrong and how to fix it
- Layouts where the next thing to read is obvious
- Spacing that groups related things and separates unrelated things

> If someone has to think about how to use your interface, you've already failed on some level. Not catastrophically — but somewhere, something could be clearer.

## The clarity test

A simple test I run on my own work: if I showed this to someone who'd never seen it before and watched them use it silently, where would they hesitate? Where would they look confused? Those moments of friction are where the design is failing.

You can't run this test on yourself — you're too familiar with the thing you built. Find someone who isn't, and watch them without helping. It's uncomfortable and invaluable.

## Clarity and beauty

I want to be clear (appropriately) that I'm not arguing against visual beauty. Typography, color, spacing, motion — these matter enormously. But they serve clarity; they don't substitute for it. A beautifully typeset page that's hard to navigate is still a failure. A plain-looking interface that's effortless to use is still a success.

Chase clarity first. The beauty tends to follow.

A collection of lessons, observations, and ideas gathered over the past twelve months — technical and otherwise.

## Looking back

Every year I try to sit down and catalog what I actually learned — not what I shipped or accomplished, but what genuinely shifted in how I think. Here's this year's list.

## Constraints are creative fuel

I spent the first half of this year frustrated by limitations: a tight budget, a legacy codebase, a stubborn stakeholder. By the middle of the year I'd realized I was approaching it wrong. Constraints don't block creativity — they focus it. Some of my best work came out of conditions I would have avoided if I'd had the choice.

## Slow down to go fast

The projects where I moved slowest at the start — spending more time planning, questioning assumptions, writing things down — were the ones that shipped fastest and with the fewest late surprises. The rush-to-build instinct is almost always wrong.

> Speed is not the same as progress. Moving fast on the wrong thing is just efficient waste.

## Teach what you're learning

I ran a small internal workshop on accessibility this year. Preparing for it taught me more than six months of passive reading had. The act of explaining something — anticipating questions, finding the right analogies — is one of the best learning tools I know.

## The smaller things

- Good documentation is a form of generosity
- `console.log` is still a valid debugging tool and anyone who disagrees is wrong
- Most meetings can be emails; most emails can be a comment in a doc
- Walking is underrated as a thinking tool

---

What did you learn this year? I'd genuinely love to know — reach out via email or find me on X.

Looking back

Every year I try to sit down and catalog what I actually learned — not what I shipped or accomplished, but what genuinely shifted in how I think. Here’s this year’s list.

Constraints are creative fuel

I spent the first half of this year frustrated by limitations: a tight budget, a legacy codebase, a stubborn stakeholder. By the middle of the year I’d realized I was approaching it wrong. Constraints don’t block creativity — they focus it. Some of my best work came out of conditions I would have avoided if I’d had the choice.

Slow down to go fast

The projects where I moved slowest at the start — spending more time planning, questioning assumptions, writing things down — were the ones that shipped fastest and with the fewest late surprises. The rush-to-build instinct is almost always wrong.

Speed is not the same as progress. Moving fast on the wrong thing is just efficient waste.

Teach what you’re learning

I ran a small internal workshop on accessibility this year. Preparing for it taught me more than six months of passive reading had. The act of explaining something — anticipating questions, finding the right analogies — is one of the best learning tools I know.

The smaller things

  • Good documentation is a form of generosity
  • console.log is still a valid debugging tool and anyone who disagrees is wrong
  • Most meetings can be emails; most emails can be a comment in a doc
  • Walking is underrated as a thinking tool

What did you learn this year? I’d genuinely love to know — reach out via email or find me on X.
The Tools I Use Every Day
A straightforward list of the tools, apps, and utilities I rely on daily — no affiliate links, no fluff, just the honest stack.

## Why bother sharing this?

I always find other people's tool lists interesting — not because I want to copy their setup, but because the choices reveal something about how they think and work. Here's mine, as of mid-2024.

## Design

**Figma** is still my primary design tool. I've tried the alternatives. They're not there yet for my workflow. I use it for everything from rough wireframes to polished components.

For quick diagrams and whiteboarding I use **Excalidraw**. It's rough on purpose, which means nobody wastes time bikeshedding the visuals in early-stage conversations.

## Development

**VS Code** with a minimal plugin set. I used to have 40 extensions; now I have six. The bloat wasn't worth the features.

**Warp** for the terminal. The autocomplete and history search alone make it worth switching from iTerm.

> The best tool is the one you stop thinking about. If you're thinking about your tool, it's getting in the way.

## Writing & notes

**Obsidian** for personal notes and a second brain. Plain markdown files, stored locally. I'm mildly paranoid about cloud lock-in with things I write, and Obsidian respects that.

For drafting longer pieces (like this one), I switch to **iA Writer**. The focused environment genuinely helps.

## Everything else

- **Arc** — browser. Spaces changed how I organise my browser context
- **Linear** — project tracking. The best-designed project management tool I've used
- **Raycast** — launcher. Replaced Alfred and Spotlight completely
- **Cleanshot X** — screenshots and screen recording. Indispensable

That's genuinely it. Every tool is something else to maintain, update, and learn. Fewer, better tools have made me calmer and more focused.

A short introduction to what you can expect from this space going forward.

## Hello, World

Welcome to my personal blog. This is a space where I'll share my thoughts on design, code, and the craft of building things for the web. If you've landed here, you probably know me already — but if not, hello. I'm glad you're here.

I've been meaning to start writing publicly for a while. The usual excuses applied: not enough time, nothing interesting to say, who would even read it. None of those were really true, so here we are.

## What to expect

I'll be writing about a few things:

- **Design** — interface design, typography, the small decisions that shape how things feel
- **Code** — front-end patterns, tools, whatever I'm learning or building at the time
- **Process** — how I think about creative work, how I stay focused, how I avoid overcomplicating things

> I write to think, not to perform. Expect unfinished ideas alongside more polished essays.

## A note on cadence

I won't commit to a posting schedule. I'll write when I have something worth saying. Quality over cadence — I'd rather publish one good thing a month than five forgettable ones a week.

If you'd like to follow along, there's an RSS feed linked in the footer.

---
